bins.name: FERC lacks jurisdiction over load
bins.desc: The Public Staff doubts FERC has authority to regulate transmission-connected load and argues most authority is reserved to the states. It says a federal rule cannot grant a right to retail service and cannot reach the generation shortage that is the real constraint, so it urges great caution if any rule is issued.
quotes: the Public Staff urges the Commission to enter into regulation of transmission-connected load with great caution, if at all.
it is doubtful that the Commission has authority to order the reforms outlined in the ANOPR, and if it does, its authority is quite narrow.
this is an area in which most, if not all, of the authority has been reserved to the states by Congress.

A Commission rule prescribing the methods, timing, and transmission pricing for interconnecting load to the transmission system could not, at the end of the day, provide any right for that load to be actually served as a retail customer, a matter unquestionably reserved to the states alone.

Because federal jurisdiction does not extend to facilities used for the generation of electric energy, the rules envisioned by the ANOPR cannot reach the heart of the problem they wish to address. A transmission study of a load that cannot be served is of no value.

bins.name: No federal right to be served
bins.desc: A federal interconnection rule cannot confer a right for a large load to actually be served as a retail customer, which is reserved to the states alone.
quotes: A Commission rule prescribing the methods, timing, and transmission pricing for interconnecting load to the transmission system could not, at the end of the day, provide any right for that load to be actually served as a retail customer, a matter unquestionably reserved to the states alone.

bins.name: No section 206 unjust-rate showing
bins.desc: The filing argues the ANOPR lacks the substantial record evidence of unjust and unreasonable rates that section 206 requires. It distinguishes Order No. 888, noting utilities already have economic incentives to serve large loads and the ANOPR never shows the rules would lower rates.
quotes: to proceed under section 206, the Commission must have substantial record evidence that the existing rate is unjust and unreasonable and that the replacement tariff is just and reasonable. However, the ANOPR does not enumerate any such practices.

rather than having an incentive to discriminate against large load customers, the economic incentives for utilities favor interconnecting large loads. The ANOPR has not articulated how the proposed rules would result in lower electric rates.

bins.name: Protect state authority and customers
bins.desc: The Public Staff warns the rules could create a new customer class with unduly preferential treatment and asks that any rule place no constraints on state authority over retail rates, generation, and siting. It supports preserving states' ability to use large-load tariffs and flexibility requirements.
quotes: the proposed rules risk creating a new class of customer that would receive unduly preferential treatment.

any final rule should place no constraints, express or implied, on the authority of states to set the terms and rates for retail customers over generation facilities, or over the siting of transmission.
limit[ing] States’ ability to implement pro ability to implement protective measures, such as large load tariffs and flexibility requirements

bins.name: Joint study delays generation
bins.desc: Studying large loads jointly with generation would add a layer that causes restudies, delays, and inaccurate studies as projects drop out or change profiles. The filing warns this could reverse progress on generation interconnection, which is the system's driving need.
quotes: Because federal jurisdiction does not extend to facilities used for the generation of electric energy, the rules envisioned by the ANOPR cannot reach the heart of the problem they wish to address. A transmission study of a load that cannot be served is of no value.

adding a new layer to the study process will inevitably cause restudies, delays, and inaccurate studies when projects inevitably either drop out of the queue or seek material changes to their load profiles.

bins.name: Joint load-generation study concerns
bins.desc: Adding load into the generation study process risks restudies and delays when load projects drop out or seek material changes, undermining the value of the studies.
quotes: adding a new layer to the study process will inevitably cause restudies, delays, and inaccurate studies when projects inevitably either drop out of the queue or seek material changes to their load profiles.

bins.name: Apply only above 300 MW
bins.desc: If FERC proceeds, the Public Staff recommends limiting any rule to loads above 300 MW to reduce the number of customers studied, focus on bulk-system impacts, and lessen jurisdictional concerns.
quotes: the North Carolina Public Staff recommends that if the Commission promulgates a rule on large load interconnection, that it limit its applicability to loads in excess of 300 MW.

bins.name: No federal limits on siting
bins.desc: Any final rule should not constrain state authority over the siting of transmission or over generation facilities serving retail customers.
quotes: any final rule should place no constraints, express or implied, on the authority of states to set the terms and rates for retail customers over generation facilities, or over the siting of transmission.

bins.name: Curtailment accommodation intrudes on states
bins.desc: The ANOPR's special accommodation for load that agrees to be curtailed quickly infringes state jurisdiction. The filing wants states to keep their ability to set flexibility requirements rather than have FERC impose them.
quotes: The ANOPR proposal to provide special accommodation to load that agrees to be curtailed is an example of a rule that quickly infringes on state jurisdiction.
limit[ing] States’ ability to implement pro ability to implement protective measures, such as large load tariffs and flexibility requirements

bins.name: Preserve bundled retail rates
bins.desc: Bundled retail rates that include transmission should be preserved as they were under Order No. 888. The Public Staff says this gives consumer advocates the transparency they need to review and audit monopoly transmission costs as those costs rise.
quotes: Bundled retail rates that include transmission should be preserved.
statutory consumer advocates need to retain this tool to ensure they can fully perform their obligation to review and audit monopoly utility costs.

bins.name: Wait for NERC guidelines
bins.desc: The Public Staff agrees all NERC reliability standards must be met and notes NERC's Large Loads Task Force is nearing completion of draft guidelines. It argues issuing final rules before those guidelines are finalized would be counterproductive.
quotes: issuing final rules prior to those guidelines being finalized would be counterproductive.
